chapters.title: Machines in the city: surveillance, convenience, and hidden costs
chapters.text:
Picture a few ordinary minutes in a major city. A scuffle between rival sports fans gets captured on an ATM security camera. A sanitation worker's GPS tracker feeds route data to city hall while residents check live updates on their phones. A tourist slips a book into a jacket pocket after leaving a shop; inventory software will later flag the missing title. Privacy barely survives a single block walk. Cameras, fitness trackers, registers, and networked devices log nearly everything we do in public and much of what we do in private.

At most points in history, nobody could observe this many simultaneous events across a metropolis. Today, software and hardware trail us constantly. Smartphones mediate dating, work, entertainment, and rest. The device sits in pockets and palms for most waking hours, smoothing logistics while draining spontaneity and warmth from daily life. In 2005, researchers asked strangers to empty their bags. Photos, religious icons, charms, and cash appeared. A decade later, the phone had absorbed those roles. Progress explains part of that shift; dependency explains the rest.

We struggle to imagine daily life without digital convenience. Yet friendly interfaces conceal opaque systems. Adam Greenfield wrote Radical Technologies to examine those systems from multiple angles—not only the polished stories manufacturers tell when selling products.

Our lives depend on technology, but we are not aware of the structure of most of the devices we encounter.

This summary tours striking technologies and ordinary ones—from pocket computers to artificial intelligence—and weighs their promises against their harms. You will ask how radical tools reshape identity and whether humans can preserve agency as machines grow more capable.

chapters.title: Voice assistants listen longer than they help
chapters.text:
Voice assistants inhabit phones, laptops, and tablets like genies bound to hardware. Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and Apple built systems that parse spoken commands, fetch answers, or launch apps. Most use female names and voices; North American research suggests users trust women's voices more readily than men's. Apple ships Siri, Amazon Alexa, while Google named its assistant after the company and avoided gendered branding.

Several problems follow widespread adoption:
• Habitual use trains impatience when responses lag even slightly.
• We outsource judgment about needs to software.
• Devices may listen continuously, not only when we invoke them.

The more we speak to machines, the faster we expect instant gratification—and the more we delegate ordinary decisions to algorithms that never sleep.

Amazon's Dash Button orders products with one press after you link an account and app. Purchases speed up; cancellations remain possible. Amazon gains addresses, phone numbers, browsing patterns, and purchase history—fuel for behavioral models, targeted ads, and promotional pings.

Any device connected to the network is bait, and the market is the fisherman.

Behind seamless checkout lies labor most shoppers never see. At Amazon fulfillment centers, summer heat can reach dangerous levels because doors stay shut to deter theft. Warehouse staff report elevated rates of illness. Comfortable one-click buying sits atop that hidden cost.

Home assistants or outdoor devices harvest data aggressively. Friendly voices simulate care without providing it. Who owns the information we scatter online remains unsettled. You can limit assistants, share data only with vendors you trust, and treat convenience as a trade—not a default. Preserving autonomy may mean speaking to humans again for tasks a device handles in seconds.

chapters.title: What a smartphone purchase actually buys
chapters.text:
Smartphones simplify and complicate life at once. On one hand, a compact phone contains a player, a notebook, a camera, and even a telephone booth. One object replaces a music player, notebook, camera, and payphone booth. A tap summons rides, messages, or maps. Meanwhile rituals fade—handwritten cards, hailing cabs with a raised arm. People surrender customs when friction drops, yet a dead battery or lost signal can trigger panic.

Screen sizes grow with each release. Touch interfaces dominate attention: scrolling, pinching, tapping. Engineers optimize usability while knowing larger glass signals status. Buyers demand storage even when price climbs because the phone holds photos, messages, and documents that feel like personal archives.

Although big storage increases the price of a smartphone, we are willing to pay more to keep our entire lives in it.

GPS and orbiting satellites let the device report location constantly. Digital maps feel miraculous until navigation becomes impossible without them. Dependence breeds helplessness; disorientation can feel like the world turned hostile overnight. The inability to orient yourself without a glowing rectangle can make a familiar street feel suddenly alien.

Regardless of where the smartphone was developed, there is a possibility that it was assembled in a factory with low wages and a high suicide rate among workers. Raw materials travel from mines in Congo; mining waste has destroyed large shares of coral reef habitat and continues to pollute waterways.

Reasonable consumption matters. Resist upgrading when hardware still works. Periodic breaks from screens curb social-media compulsion and remind you that life offline still functions. The prospect of a new model is seductive, but a working phone already meets most needs. Time away from smartphones helps prevent addiction to social networks and restores attention you did not know you had lost.

chapters.title: Augmented reality cannot split your attention across two worlds
chapters.text:
In summer 2016, Shayla Wiggins followed a Pokémon Go prompt toward a river and found a man's body in the water. The case might have lingered unsolved longer without augmented reality pulling her outdoors.

Virtual reality seals you inside headsets and headphones with simulated scenes. Augmented reality layers digital content onto physical space, enlarging perception. Designers once pitched AR for memory aids—recognizing faces for people who struggle with names—yet holding a phone to a friend's face to pull up a profile hardly improves rapport.

AR entertains but can endanger users. Walking with a screen thrust forward invites theft and disorientation. Players once searched for creatures at the 9/11 memorial, provoking outrage. Extra layers confirm a simple truth: you cannot fully inhabit two places at once.

Philip K. Dick once defined reality as "that which, when you stop believing in it, doesn't go away," and it's this bedrock quality of universal accessibility—to anyone at all, at any time of his or her choosing—that constitutes reality's primary virtue. ~ Adam Greenfield

AR positions itself as mediator, implying ordinary life needs enhancement. Where augmented reality was supposed to be an assistant, inconsistencies arise, and sometimes the information it broadcasts turns out to be incorrect. If you open walk mode on Google Maps, you may see venues already closed and out of date. Sustained AR use scatters focus and may worsen attention disorders. Pokémon Go correlated with injuries and fatalities as players lost vigilance.

Thus, the additional reality confirms that it is impossible to exist in two places simultaneously. Augmented reality is fascinating as entertainment, but it entails the danger of being robbed when a person moves with the phone extended forward. Before diving into virtual worlds, ground yourself in the one you already occupy.

chapters.title: Cash still beats cryptocurrency for everyday life
chapters.text:
Satoshi Nakamoto—the pseudonym behind Bitcoin—remains unidentified. A 2008 cryptography mailing-list paper introduced the concept. Stylometric analysis hints at British English, little more. The inventor's anonymity only deepens the myth around digital cash.

Bitcoin aimed to replace cash with fast peer payments. Earlier digital money required trusting intermediaries who saw transaction details and sometimes failed to settle trades cleanly. Some transactions occurred in multiples while others never completed at all.

Bitcoin's ledger gave each user identifiers and chained blocks of activity, promising traceable ownership without a central clerk. Each action becomes a digital code; each subsequent block contains information from the previous one, forming a blockchain that acts as database, encryption layer, and transfer protocol.

But why do we still not pay with bitcoins at every step and do not see bitcoin price tags in stores? Firstly, this currency is extremely unstable. Volatility terrifies savers—peaks near sixty thousand dollars collapsed toward triple digits. Small transfers can take about ten minutes; large amounts may exceed an hour.

You cannot pay for tickets for flights on well-known airlines using cryptocurrency. Starting and replenishing a bitcoin wallet is not easy, and finding someone who accepts payments in cryptocurrency is even more difficult. Gum and bottled water at corner stores stay cash-or-card affairs. Mining burns enormous electricity; server farms vent heat at industrial scale. Unfortunately, cryptocurrency does not justify the number of resources spent on it. Mining cryptocurrency burns a lot of energy, and farms generate problematic amounts of heat. Climate risk stays concrete while everyday benefits stay fuzzy. While the benefits of Bitcoins are still blurred, the threat of global warming remains very real.

Cryptocurrency is promising, but you should consider whether you need its benefits now, not in the future.

Did you know? By 2015, the British government estimated Bitcoin's daily energy draw comparable to Ireland's national electricity use.

chapters.title: Automation, robots, and the shrinking job market
chapters.text:
Machines outmuscle humans, never tire, and skip boredom. Economist John Maynard Keynes coined "technological unemployment" in 1928, warning automation could erase most human jobs in the future.

Building thinking machines marks scientific triumph and economic threat. The creation of intelligent machines is not only progress, which can make life easier; it is also a real challenge for the human intellect. A device matching human reasoning would rank among history's defining inventions. Employers also prize machines that skip overtime pay and complaints. For management, progress often means compliant labor that never negotiates conditions.

Automation already reshapes service work. McDonald's rolled out self-order kiosks in 2014, claiming staff would shift to "higher-value" tasks while a former U.S. division head argued robots cost less than raising wages for "inefficient" employees. Keikyu Corporation in Japan began scoring employees' smile "quality" in 2009.

This approach looks depressing: boutique owners may swap consultants for robots immune to burnout. Although the attempt to replace people with robots at boring or hard jobs seems noble, it hides the fact that millions of people who do not have sufficient qualifications or documents for other positions will be left without sources of income.

Hard times are coming for those who have nothing to offer the economy but their muscle, their heart, or their sex. ~ Adam Greenfield

There will hardly be at least one task that a machine cannot perform in the future, which will change the economy, society's organization, and our perception of ourselves. We mostly associate automated systems with something good that will help people, not replace them; we see a future with mixed teams of people and robots. However, our desire to completely trust machines can play a cruel joke.

We should value people, not for their work but the simple fact of their existence.

chapters.title: Machine-made art may hang in galleries but rarely moves the heart
chapters.text:
Algorithms now tackle harder perception tasks—tracking faces through hairstyle changes or spotting cups amid shattered fragments on the floor.

Training does not always succeed, and it is too early to talk about unambiguous success. Tesla shipped Autopilot in 2015; Elon Musk promised self-steering, speed control, and parking while fleet data trained networks. In May 2016 Joshua Brown died when his car failed to distinguish a white truck from bright sky. No matter how quickly machines learn, it is still too early to give them complete control.

Important inventions sometimes take lives.

Some dismiss inspiration as myth, arguing machines can master color, melody, or plot as readily as pattern recognition. However, human jazz singers inject the pain and strength of their life experiences into their songs; zeroes and ones cannot express such emotions.

The developers of The Next Rembrandt had their own opinion. They created a 3D-printed Rembrandt portrait with the texture of an oil painting on canvas. The program analyzed hundreds of authentic canvases and 3D-printed a new portrait mimicking brush texture, line, and chiaroscuro. Technically, experts recreated his typical lines and contrast of light and dark—but there were no real artist's life experiences, mood, or soul in the image.

When we see technically perfect art, we like it. But when the artist gives their soul to art — we love it.

New talented artists born in the age of digital production risk living unnoticed. Machine-made paintings are difficult to evaluate because they are competent by definition. Their works may be in museums, but such art is not eternal.

We feel delighted with each news about the achievements of technology. The greater the success of artificial intelligence, the greater the human possibilities seem to us. But at the same time, we will feel a constant sense of pressure that machines could replace the talents of humans so quickly.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
We cannot completely hide from technology even if we live in a forest: satellites or electric wires will silently do their work even there. Algorithms will suggest cafés; children will chase AR creatures in parks. Technology surrounds us—complex, consequential, and often delightful.

The more devices enter daily routines, the more literacy about them matters. Swapping phones, adopting robot vacuums, or opening crypto wallets all demand informed consent. Learn the risks and policies of the companies whose products and services you use. Ask whether a voice assistant truly helps or merely replaces human contact—a busy parent may welcome hands-free scheduling, yet kids might prefer screens to neighborhood playmates. Approach the issue of technology wisely and implement it only where it is needed.

A new technology offering means you have a choice, not an obligation to buy it.

Blockchains, printers, and recommendation engines rarely deliver exactly what inventors imagined, yet once deployed they rewrite reality. If you want to open a cryptocurrency wallet or buy a new smartphone, you can do it without feeling guilty. But do not let automation determine all your decisions — remember that every coin has a second side, and someone's losses may turn out to be more global than your gains. We can live in harmony with technology when we do not forget our own capabilities.

Try this

Phone addiction—especially among teens—feeds anxiety through endless social feeds. Practical steps help:
• Disable push notifications.
• Avoid screens for an hour before sleep and after waking.
• Cap daily social-media time with app limits.

Constant monitoring of social media increases anxiety, so small boundaries can restore calm.
